2.1. Chemicals and reagents All solutions used for the electrochemical studies were prepared using bidistilled water. Graphite powder, sodium borohydride, sulfuric acid, sodium nitrate and tetrachloroauric (III) acid trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O) were purchased from Merck. Sodium hydroxide was purchased from Fluka.

2.3. Preparation of graphene support Graphene oxide (GO) was prepared with the Hummers method and purified [34], then chemical reduction of GO to graphene was carried out [30]. Typically, graphite powder (5 g) and NaNO3 (2.5 g) were mixed with 120 mL of H2SO4 (95%) in a three-neck 500 mL flask. The flask was put in an ice bath and the mixture was stirred. After 30 min, potassium permanganate (15 g) was added slowly under vigorous stirring to the suspension. After completing the addition of potassium permanganate,the mixture had been stirring at room temperature overnight. Then, 150 mL of H2O was slowly added to the pasty mixture under vigorous stirring. The addition of water raised the reaction temperature to 98 ◦C with effervescence. The diluted suspension had been stirring at 98 ◦C in an oil bath with reflux for 1 day. After 24 h, 50 mL of 30% H2O2 was added to the mixture. For purification, the mixture was washed by rinsing and centrifugation with 5% HCl and then with deionized (DI) water for several times until the pH became neutral. After filtration and drying, the GO was obtained. For preparing graphene, chemical reduction of GO by hydrazine was carried out. Typically, 50 mg of GO was dispersed in 20 mL DI water by ultrasonication for 1 h. Then 0.5 mL of hydrazine monohydrate was added, and the mixture had been stirring at 90 ◦C in an oil bath with reflux for 24 h. Finally, the mixture was washed by rinsing and centrifugation with DI water for several times to remove the excess hydrazine. The final product was dried in an oven and graphene was obtained as a black powder.

2.4. Preparation of electrocatalysts (HNAu/G and NAu/G) Au hollow nanospheres were synthesized using cobalt nanoparticles as sacrificial template according to Eq. (4) [2]. Typically, sodium citrate solution (2 mL of 0.1 M) and 300 mL of distilled water were added to a three-neck 500 mL flask. After deoxygenating, freshly made sodium borohydride solution (1.2 mL of 1 M) was added, and then cobalt chloride solution (0.3 mL of 0.4 M) was added drop-wise to the above solution under vigorous stirring. By addition of cobalt chloride solution, because of cobalt nanoparticles formation, the solution color became brown. After completing the addition of cobalt chloride, the solution had been stirring for 1 h while maintaining nitrogen flow, so that the sodium borohydride could be completely decomposed. After 1 h, aqueous chloroauric acid solution (15 mL of 0.005 M) was added drop-wise to the solution under magnetic stirring. At this step, the color of the solution changed from brown to a deep blue because of Au hollow nanospheres formation (nitrogen was bubbling through the solution during the whole procedure) [35]. The galvanic replacement reaction between Co nanoparticles and chloroauric is as Eq. (4). As the standard reduction potential of AuCl4 −/Au redox couple (0.994V vs. SHE) is higher than the standard reduction potential of Co2+/Co redox couple (−0.277V vs. SHE) [35], Co nanoparticles are immediately oxidized to Co2+ when mixed with an aqueous AuCl4 −. 3Co + 2AuCl4 − → 2Au + 3Co2+ + 8Cl− (4) Upon completion of gold addition, the nitrogen flow was stopped, and the mixture had been stirring for 1 h to oxidize any remaining cobalt metal in solution. After 1 h, 50 mg of asprepared graphene was added to the above solution. After stirring for 12 h, the final product, Au hollow nanospheres on graphene support (HNAu/G), was collected by filtration and washed several times with distilled water. For comparison, Au solid nanoparticles with roughly similar diameter were prepared by citrate reduction method [36]. Then the Au solid nanoparticles were dispersed on graphene supports, and the NAu/G was obtained. The schematic procedure of HNAu/G preparation is illustrated in Fig. 1.

All reagents and solvents were of analytical grade and were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich. 1,4-Bis(terpyridine-4′ -yl)benzene was synthesized using a literature technique [26]. Distilled deionized water prepared in the Milli-Q-RO4 (Millipore) system was used for the preparation of all solutions. Centrifugation was performed in an LMC-3000 centrifuge (centrifugation velocity 3000 revolutions/s). Electronic UV–vis spectra were recorded on a Hitachi U-2900 instrument. Thermogravimetry analysis (TGA) was carried out on a differential scanning calorimeter DSC 204 F1 Phoenix.

2.3. Synthesis of 1,4-bis(terpyridine-4′ -yl)benzene/gold nanoparticle composite (Au–BTB) To the 5 ml of distilled acetonitrile 27 mg of 1,4-bis (terpyridine4 ′ -yl) benzene was added, the resulting mixture was carefully suspended in an ultrasonic bath and added to 45 ml of deionized water. 63 ml of an AuNPs solution was added to the resulting solution and it was kept in the freezer at −18 ◦C for 30 min. The resulting suspension was centrifuged for 15 min at a rotation speed of 3000 revolutions/min, and the solution of unabsorbed nanoparticles was decanted. The precipitate was suspended in a small amount of deionized water, centrifuged for 15 min and decanted. Resultingnanomaterial was thensuspendedindeionized water and was stored as suspension at room temperature. The Au–BTB composite precipitate can also be dried under low pressure at room temperature for 6 h and kept as a powder.

2.4. Reduction of 4-nitrophenol (4NP) by NaBH4 catalyzed by 1,4-bis(terpyridine-4′ -yl)benzene/gold nanoparticle composite Aqueous solution of NaBH4 (0.1 ml, 0.3 M) and aqueous solution of 4NP (0.1 ml, 3 mM) were mixed with 2.8 ml of water in the quartz cell (1 cm path length), leading to a color change from light yellow to dark yellow-green. Then, Au–BTB composite (5.00, 0.50, 0.25 and 0.05 mg) was added to the mixture and it was quickly placed in the cell holder of the spectrophotometer. The progress of the conversion of 4NP to 4-aminophenol(4AP) was then monitored by UV–vis spectroscopy recording the time-dependent adsorption spectra of the reaction mixture with time intervals of 2.5 or 10 min in a spectral range of 200–700 nm at ambienttemperature. The rate constants of the reduction process were determined through measuring the change in absorbance at 400 nm as a function of time. The product was identified by 1H NMR (D2O) by comparison with an authentic sample.

2.1 Reagents and apparatus Commercial pectin (CP Kelco brand) type Genu 105 with esterification degree of 67% was used as a sample standard. HAuCl4, AgNO3, oxalic acid, trisodium citrate and methyl orange (Sigma Aldrich) were used.

2.3 Preparation of silver, gold and gold-silver nanoparticles The following solutions were prepared: HAuCl4 1mM and AgNO3 1mM. Pectin extracted from orange (OP) and commercial pectin were used to prepare the nanoparticles under the same conditions. An aqueous solution of pectin 0.5% (w/v) was prepared under stirring for two hours at 40 ºC. Solutions of sodium citrate 0.1 M and oxalic acid 0.1 M were prepared as reduction agents.

2.3.1 Gold nanoparticles In a typical synthesis, OP (1 mL) and HAuCl4 (3mL) solutions were added to distilled water (25 mL) under stirring. The resulting solution was heated at 80 ºC, and the reducing agent (1 mL of trisodium citrate or oxalic acid solutions) was added. The solution was kept boiling until colour change, then the heat source was immediately removed. In a second procedure the pH of the resulting solution was set to 11 by adding solution of NaOH 0.1 M. Afterwards, it was heated up to 80 ºC under vigorous stirring and oxalic acid solution (1 mL) was introduced. The Page 13 of 28 Accepted Manuscript solution was kept boiling until colour change, then immediately removed from the heat.

2.3.2 Silver nanoparticles Silver nanoparticles were prepared with a similar methodology used for the synthesis of gold nanoparticles mentioned before. Only trisodium citrate was used as reducing agent.

2.3.3 Pectin as reduction agent of gold OP could be used as reducing and stabilizer agent of Au NPs. OP (3 mL) and HAuCl4 (3 mL) solutions were added to distilled water (24 mL) under stirring. The solution was kept boiling until colour change, then immediately removed from the heat. On the course of the reaction, several colours changing took place on the aqueous solutions, whose characteristic extinction bands were carefully monitored by UV-Vis spectrophotometry.

2.3.4 Au-Ag alloy nanoparticles Au-Ag alloy nanoparticles were prepared by modifying the Ag:Au molar ratio. In this case, only trisodium citrate was used as reduction agent. The solutions of OP (1 mL), AgNO3 and HAuCl4, whose Ag:Au volume ratio was set to 3:1, 1:1 or 1:3, were added to distilled water. The final volume was 30 mL. They were heated at 80 ºC and trisodium citrate solution (1 mL) was added. The solution was kept boiling until colour change, then immediately removed from the heat.

2.1 Chemicals and Synthesis Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4 aq) 99.999% grade was purchased from Aldrich. Biograde sodium citrate was from Sigma-Aldrich. 10 mM phosphate buffer (PB) pH 7.4 was used. Poly(ethylene glycol) with a disulphide linkage (P8735-EG-disulfide, Mw 6500 g/mol), hereafter named EG-S-S, was from Polymer Source Inc. Quebec, Canada (Scheme 1c). The polymers pTHMMAA and Lipa-pTHMMAA were prepared according to the method by Albers et al. (Mw in the range of 4500 and 6000 g/mol) [25,28]. The compound Lipa-MF was synthesized according to the following procedure: To a mixture of 1,1-dimethylbiguanide (1.15 g, 8.87 mmol) in acetonitrile (50 mL) was added a mixture of lipoic acid NHS ester (1.0 g, 3.3 mmol) in acetonitrile (50 mL). A white precipitate was formed and the mixture was stirred for 1 h. The reaction was judged completed by TLC analysis (eluent: ethyl acetate). The mixture was filtered and the clear yellow filtrate was concentrated by rotary evaporation to about 15 mL. The mixture was purified by chromatography (silica gel, eluent dichloromethane/methanol, 20/1, v/v). 2.2 Preparation of gold nanoparticles Gold nanoparticles were prepared according to the method by Frens [35]. Briefly, a 50 mL aqueous solution of HAuCl4 (0.25 mM) was heated to boiling and trisodium citrate (138 mM) was added. The solution was allowed to boil for another 20 minutes, and cooled down during continuous stirring. The resultant gold colloids were stored in 4 ◦C. The pH of the AuNP solution was adjusted to a pH of 6.4. These particles are termed “AuNPs citrate”. A set of AuNPs was centrifuged at 4oC at 4000 rpm for 30 minutes. The supernatant was discarded, phosphate buffer, PB (10 mM sodium phosphate at pH 7.4) was added and the tube was centrifuged once more. This procedure was repeated twice to change to PB buffer. These nanoparticles are termed “AuNPs PB”.

2.1. Synthesis of gold colloid nanoparticles The gold colloid nanoparticles are prepared by the sodium citrate reduction of HAuCl4 in distilled water. Spherical gold nanoparticles with 15 nm in diameter are synthesized under reflux condition. The reflux environment is used to maintain constant density of gold nanoparticles in the colloid.

2.1. Synthesis of gold colloid nanoparticles The gold colloid nanoparticles are prepared by the sodium citrate reduction of HAuCl4 in distilled water. Spherical gold nanoparticles with 15 nm in diameter are synthesized under reflux condition. The reflux environment is used to maintain constant density of gold nanoparticles in the colloid.

All starting materials were commercially purchased and used without further purification. Milli-Q water with the resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm was used throughout the experiments. Citrate-protected AuNPs (cit-AuNPs) were prepared by using citrate ion as a reducing reagent. Namely, a solution of HAuCl4 (0.010 wt% in Milli-Q water, 300 mL) was heated to the boiling temperature. Then, an aqueous solution of sodium citrate (1.0 wt%, 7.5, 3.7 or 2.4 mL) was added to the boiling solution of HAuCl4, giving cit-AuNPs (cit-AuNP/water) with the diameter of 17, 28 or 48 nm, respectively. The reaction mixture was kept at the boiling temperature for 60 min, and was allowed to cool down to room temperature. Next, hydrophobic AuNP was prepared from cit-AuNPs by a ligand-exchange process. First, 800 mg of octadecanethiol (ODT) was dissolved into 20 mL of ethanol by ultrasonic irradiation for 10 min. Subsequently, the colloidal solution (20 mL) of as-prepared cit-AuNPs with the diameter of 17, 28 or 48 nm was added to the ethanol solution of ODT under vigorous stirring for 24 h. Then, chloroform (5 mL) was added to the reaction mixture followed by sonication, in order to extract the resultant AuNPs capped with ODT (ODT-AuNPs).29 Then, the organic phase was centrifuged so as to remove excess ODT (12,000 x g, 10 min, 5 times). The ODTAuNPs were obtained as powder state after vacuum drying. A noteworthy characteristics of obtained ODT-AuNPs was that they were re-dispersed into chloroform.

ODT-AuNP films were fabricated by the LB technique. First, dried ODT-AuNPs (5.0 mg) were re-dispersed into chloroform (1.0 mL) with or without PEG of 50 ng mL-1 by sonication. PEG was used as an amphiphilic polymer which helped to spread the hydrophobic AuNPs onto water and to form uniform AuNP monolayer. Then, 200 µL solution of ODT-AuNP with or without PEG was spread on the surface of water as the 6 subphase in the LB trough (USI-3-22QY, USI, Japan) at 25℃, and kept for 15 min to evaporate the solvent. The resultant nanoparticle film was compressed at a speed of 0.1 mm s -1 , and then transferred onto an ITO substrate by LB method at a speed of 0.2 mm s-1 . The ITO substrate was beforehand cleaned by ultrasonication in water and acetone for 5 min, followed by cleaning with UV-ozone for 15 min and subsequent immersion into the boiling 2-propanol for 10 min. It was dried with nitrogen gas just before use. UV-vis spectra were recorded on JASCO V-670ST. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were performed on Hitachi SU-8000 and JEOL JEM-200CX, respectively.

2.1 Materials and Methods Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate(lll) trihydrate (HAuCl4.3H2O), sodium borohydride (NaBH4) and silver nitrate (AgNO3) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Cetyltrimethylammonium 8 bromide (CTAB), 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) and ascorbic acid were obtained from Alfa-Aesar. All chemicals were used as received and their solutions were freshly prepared in deionized water prior to each experiment.

2.2 Synthesis of PNIPAM Microgel Beads (PNIPAM) Thermo-responsive poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAM) microgel beads (crosslinking density = 4.25 mol %) were synthesized using precipitation polymerization as described in the previous studies [16]. Usually in the polymerization process, N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAM), N, N methylene-bis-acrylamide (BIS) and potassium persulphate (KPS) were used as monomer, cross-linker and initiator respectively. Briefly the polymerization was carried out in a 250 ml three-neck round bottom flask equipped with a reflux water cooling condenser and nitrogen (N2) inlet. The flask was immersed in a water bath regulated at a temperature of 70 C. At first, required amount of the prepared aqueous solution mixtures of NIPAM and BIS was transferred into a three-neck flask. The above mixture was degassed with N2 gas for 30 minutes at temperature 70 C with constant stirring speed of 250 rpm. After that KPS solution was added to the above mixture to start the polymerization. The polymerization was stopped after 6 hrs and the suspension was cooled down for overnight under slow stirring. Finally, the suspension was purified by washing several times with MiliQ water using centrifuge.

2.3 PNIPAM Stabilized Au Nanorods (PNIPAM-AuNR) A two step approach was followed for the synthesis of PNIPAM stabilized AuNR. The first step involved the seed preparation, for which about 0.3 ml of 0.01 M NaBH4 aqueous solution was added into a reaction mixture containing 0.125 ml of 0.01M HAuCl4.3H2O and 3.75 ml of 0.1 M CTAB. The seed formation was confirmed by the color alteration of the solution from light yellow to brown color. The UV-vis absorption study showed no sign of any SPR band indicating the formation of ultra-small gold nano crystals of size 2-3 nm. The second step involved the growth of AuNR on the PNIPAM microgel by seeding approach. In a typical experiment, the PNIPAM microgel was impregnated with an excess of Au seed solution followed by centrifugation to remove the unbound Au seeds, thus, containing the microgel stabilized Au seeds for further growth processes. Then, a growth solution was prepared comprising CTAB solution (5 ml, 0.1 M), HAuCl4.3H2O (0.25 ml, 0.01 M), AgNO3 (40 l, 0.01 M) and ascorbic acid (50 l, 0.1 M) to which added the PNIPAM stabilized Au seeds. The only difference was that the growth solution prepared here favored the growth of AuNR. The progress of the reaction was visualized by color change from colourless to purple 30 min after PNIPAM stabilized Au seeds addition indicating AuNR formation. The growth of AuNR over Au seeds stabilized on the PNIPAM microgel was allowed for 24 hrs which was followed by controlled centrifugation to get pure AuNR stabilized with PNIPAM microgel.

2.4 Synthesis of PNIPAM Stabilized Au Nanospheres (PNIPAM-AuNS) The PNIPAM stabilized Au Nanoparticles were prepared following the seed mediated approach. The first step involved the seed preparation, for which about 0.3 ml of 0.01 M NaBH4 10 aqueous solution was added into a reaction mixture containing 0.125 ml of 0.01 M HAuCl4.3H2O and 3.75 ml of 0.1 M CTAB. The seed formation was confirmed by the color alteration of the solution from light yellow to brown color. The UV-vis absorption study showed no sign of any SPR band indicating the formation of ultra-small gold nanocrystals of size 2-3 nm. The second step involved the growth of AuNS on the PNIPAM microgel by seeding approach. In a typical experiment, the PNIPAM microgel was impregnated with an excess of Au seed solution followed by centrifugation to remove the unbound Au seeds, thus, containing the microgel stabilized Au seeds for further growth processes. Then, a growth solution was prepared comprising CTAB solution (5 ml, 0.1 M), HAuCl4.3H2O (0.25 ml, 0.01 M) and ascorbic acid (50 l, 0.1 M) to which added the PNIPAM stabilized Au seeds. The progress of the reaction was visualized by color change from pale yellow to ruby red 30 min after Au seed addition indicating AuNS formation. The growth of AuNS over Au seeds stabilized on the PNIPAM microgel was allowed for 24 hrs which was followed by controlled centrifugation to get pure AuNS stabilized with PNIPAM microgel

2.5 Synthesis of PNIPAM Stabilized Ag Nanospheres (PNIPAM-AgNS) The synthesis of PNIPAM stabilized AgNS involved the conventional reduction reaction at room temperature. Typically, the PNIPAM microgel beads were first dispersed in 1.5 ml distilled water followed by addition of 0.2 ml of 0.005 M AgNO3 solution. To the above reaction mixture added 0.2 ml of 0.005 M NaBH4 aqueous solution under constant stirring, which continued for another 15 minutes to assure completion of reaction. The progress of the reaction was visualized by colour change from colourless to pale yellow immediately after borohydride addition indicating AgNS formation. The resultant PNIPAM-AgNS were washed twice with 11 distilled water by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 15 minutes providing purified PNIPAM stabilized AgNS.

2.1. Chemicals and materials Auric chloride (HAuCl4), Cystine, Melamine, Glutathione (GSH), Alanine, Glycine, and Glucose were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Sodium borohydride (NaBH4) was purchased from Merck. Ethanol was purchased from SD fine chemicals, India. The chemicals were used as received withoutfurther purification and all solutions were prepared with MilliQ water (18.2 M cm).

2.3. Synthesis of AuNCs The glutathione protected gold nanoclusters were synthesized by a procedure slightly modified from previously reported method [21]. Briefly, 1 mM of HAuCl4 was added dropwise to 10 mL of 5 mM glutathione aqueous solution under constant stirring and the white color precipitate was formed. Few drops of 1 mM NaBH4 aqueous solution was slowly added to the mixture. The color of the solution gradually turned from white to brownish red and the stirring was continued for another one hour. The reaction mixture was centrifuged with MilliQ water and Ethanol at three times each to remove unreacted molecules and impurities. The obtained gold nanoclusters precipitate was dispersed in 10 mL of MilliQ water for further characterizations and applications.

2.1 Materials and chemicals Glass dishes were cut into small rectangular flakes with length × width of 8 mm × 8 mm. Glass flakes were cleaned by using an aqueous 20% RBS detergent solution (in water) in ultrasonic bath for 10 min and then were rinsed by deionized water before the samples were dried by compressed pressure air. APTES (NH2 (CH2 ) 3 –Si(OC2H5 ) 3 , 98%) was purchased from VWR, gold(III) chloride hydrate (HAuCl4 ·xH2O, 99.999%) and silver nitrate (AgNO3 , 99%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, trisodium citrate (HOC(COONa)(CH₂COONa)₂·2H2O, 99%) was purchased from Alfa(Aesar). Ethanol and acetone were analytic reagent grade. Pure Ar was used for generating plasma; its purity was higher than 99.99%.

2.4 Synthesis and immobilization of AuNps Monodisperse gold nanopaticles were synthesized according to the work of Xia [29]. In a test tube of 0.5 mL of an aqueous solution of gold(III) chloride hydrate (1 8 wt%), 42 µL of aqueous silver nitrate solution (0.1 wt% ) and 1.5mL of aqueous sodium citrate (1 wt%) were mixed. The mixture was injected into 47.5 mL of boiling water under reflux which resulted in the formation of a red sol of gold NPs. Heating was stopped after 30min and the suspension was cooled to room temperature. The pH of the suspension was adjusted to 6.2 using with NaOH (0.1 M). Glass substrates with freshly deposited APTES were then placed at the bottom of beaker for 24 h, magnetic rotation bar was used during immobilization.

2.1 Synthesis of SiO2 colloidal particle arrays Firstly, monodisperse SiO2 solid spheres were synthesized by the Stöber method.20 24.75 mL of H2O, and 9.0 mL of ammonium hydroxide were rapidly added into a mixture of 4.5 mL of Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) and 61.75 mL of ethanol. The solution was kept stirring at 40 ℃ for 2 h. The particle sizes of the SiO2 spheres were controlled by changing the concentration of ammonium and the quantity of TEOS. Then, the SiO2 colloidal spheres were slowly self-organized into highly ordered colloidal arrays to form opal photonic crystals (OPCs) on the glass substrate, driven by surface tension of the liquid in the evaporating process.

2.2 Synthesis of the AuNS The gold nanostars (AuNS) were synthesized by the seed-mediated method.21 And, the spherical gold seed was prepared according to the citrate reduction method. The 8 ml of 1 mM HAuCl4 aqueous solution was added to 24 ml of deionized water and stirred heating under nitrogen protection. When the solution was boiled, 900 μl of 1 w/v% sodium citrate solution was added immediately and kept at boiling until it became wine red solution. Finally, the seeds were obtained when the solution was cooled down. And they were used for a day whose color became more intense. Then, 2.5 ml of 1 mM HAuCl4 aqueous solution was added to 7 ml of deionized water. A certain amount of Au seed, 22 μl of 1% sodium citrate and 1 ml of 30 mM hydroquinone were added in order. This solution was kept stirring at room temperature for 30 min to obtain AuNS particles. Finally, the products were centrifuged for 15 min at 10000 rpm and washed with ethonal three times, and then redispersed into deionized water. It is worth noting that the size of AuNS was controlled by varying the amount of gold seed in this experiment.

Tri-sodium citrate, hydrogen peroxide, sulphuric acid, ethanol and acetone were purchased from EMerck (Germany) and were used without further purification. Spectral grade chloroform was purchased from SRL, India and was used as collected. All the glasswares used in the synthesis were prepared after cleaning with freshly prepared aqua-regia and then rinsed thoroughly with distilled water prior to use. Triple distilled deionized water with 18.2 MΩ cm electrical resistivity and pH ~6.8, as obtained from MilliQ-plus system of Millipore Corporation, USA was used for preparation of all the required solutions used in the experiment

Quasi spherical gold nanocolloids (AuNcs) of average particle diameter ~55 nm were synthesized by citrate reduction method as reported by Frens [29]. Briefly, an amount of 50 ml aqueous solution of HAucl4 (0.25 M) and 300 μl trisodium citrate (1%) was heated up to boiling with vigorous stirring. The color of the gold colloidal solution turns from deep blue to reddish pink in the process of reduction. The stable reddish pink color indicates the formation of Au nanocolloids. EDX spectra of FESEM images shown in Fig. S1 (Supplementary Material) confirm the existence of gold in Au nanocolloidal solution. HR TEM image of the as synthesized nanoparticle is displayed in Fig. S2. Fig. S2 shows the formation of quasi spherical gold nanocolloids with average particle diameter ~55 nm.

2.1 Materials HAuCl4·xH2O (~50 Au basis units), sodium citrate tribasic dehydrate (>99%), 1- octadecanethiol (98%), Rhodamine 6G (R6G, 99%), and hexamethyldisilazane (99.9%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Acetone (99.5%) and isopropyl alcohol (99.5%) were provided 6 by PanReac. Hexane (95%) and chloroform (99%) were supplied by Fisher Chemicals and Acros Organics, respectively. All solutions were prepared in Milli-pore MilliQ water (resistivity 18.2 MΩ·cm). 4” polished SiO2/Si wafers from Sil´Tronix (wet thermal oxide 1 µm thick) were used as substrates onto which the Langmuir-Schaeffer films were transferred.

2.2 Synthesis of AuNPs AuNPs were prepared using a method described previously.[9] Briefly, HAuCl4·xH2O (19 mg) was dissolved in MilliQ water (50 mL) at 70 ºC. Afterward, preheated sodium citrate (5 mL, 38 × 10-3 M) was added dropwise to the first solution under magnetic stirring. The final solution was kept at 70 ºC during 10 min, showing a red-wine color. Then, the AuNPs dispersion was cooled to room temperature and stored at 4 ºC. Octadecylthiolate protected AuNPs (AuNP-C18) were prepared at room temperature by a two-phase liquid-liquid system based on the method reported by Brust et al.[22] 15 mL of an aqueous solution of AuNPs were mixed with a solution of 1-octadecanethiol in hexane (7 g in 30 mL of hexane). The two-phase mixture was stirred vigorously until the AuNP-C18 dispersion was transferred to the organic phase. The organic phase completely changed color from colorless to blue in 45 minutes (see Figure 1). Then, the mixture was added to a separating flask. The organic phase was kept and a fresh solution of 1-octadecanethiol (1 g in 15 mL of hexane) was added to the aqueous phase. After stirring for 20 min, the organic phase was separated again. All the organic solutions were mixed and centrifuged (3 cycles at 11,000 rpm during 15 min) in order to remove the non-adsorbed octadecanethiol molecules. Finally, the centrifugalized pellet was redispersed into two different solvents (hexane and chloroform) and mixtures of them in several ratios. The final gold concentration in the AuNP-C18 dispersion is 1.1 mg·mL-1 as determined by the amount of solvent infused and assuming both: a yield of 100% in the ligand exchange process and no loss of material in the recovery process. In order to define the most suitable conditions for the fabrication of homogeneous and tightly-packed films of AuNP-C18, experimental studies were carried out under different hexane:chloroform volumetric ratios of the spreading solution, i.e. 1:0, 1:1, 1:3, 1:5, 1:7 and 0:1. The dispersions of the gold nanoparticles were named as “AuNP-C18_hexane:chloroform solvent ratio”, for instance: AuNP-C18_1:5 for AuNPs-C18 dispersed in a hexane:chloroform 1:5 solvent mixture.

Materials Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4 ), bovine serum albumin, fraction V (BSA) and acetone (reagent grade) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich Co. LLC. RPMI 1640 medium supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin-streptomycin (Pen-strep), and EDTA were purchased from Gibco, Life Technologies.

Synthesis of BSA Au clusters The Au nanoclusters used in this study were synthesized using an approach developed by Xie et al.11. Typically, 5 mL of 10 mM HAuCl4 was mixed with 5 mL of 50 mg/mL BSA and incubated overnight at 37°C. The light brown cluster solution was further dialyzed (2000 MWCO membrane, Slide-A-lyzer, dialysis cassette, Thermo Scientific) against de-ionized water for at least 12 hr with periodic change of water to remove any small impurities. The dialyzed cluster solution was filtered using a 0.02 µm syringe filter and then used for subsequent measurements.

Highly monodisperse poly(glycidyl methacrylate) colloidal
particles with diameter of 448  9 nm were synthesized by a
modied surfactant-free emulsion polymerization method
introduced in the literature.17 Briey, 15 mL of inhibitor-free
glycidyl methacrylate (GMA) ($97.0%, Sigma-Aldrich, St Louis,
MO, USA) was dispersed in 150 mL of Nanopure water (Barnstead, 18 MU cm) purged with N2 by stirring vigorously at 1200
rpm for 30 min at room temperature. The mixture was reuxed
and heated to 90 C following dropwise addition of 0.5 g of
potassium persulfate (KPS) (99+%, Sigma-Aldrich, St Louis, MO,
USA). The ow of nitrogen was then decreased through a
nitrogen tank pressure regulator to minimize the stripping of
monomer from the reaction mixture. The stirring rate of 1200
rpm and temperature of 90 C were kept constant until the end
of the reaction. Aer 2 h of reaction, the reaction mixture was
terminated by bubbling oxygen for 30 min. Upon completion of
the reaction, the product was allowed to cool to room temperature. 10 mL of the PGMA spheres were dialyzed with Cellulose
Ester Dialysis membrane (MWCO 3500–5000, Spectrum Laboratory, Inc, Rancho Dominguez, CA) for 24 h and centrifuged at
5000 rpm (Eppendorf centrifuge 5804, VWR International,
Radnor, PA, USA) for 30 min. Aer three successive centrifugation/wash cycles with Nanopure water, 11 mL of puried
PGMA spheres were obtained. Weak hydrophobic polyelectrolyte was adsorbed onto the PGMA spheres by the deposition technique, using positively charged PAH solution
(Mw 15 000, Sigma-Aldrich, St Louis, MO, USA) (Scheme 1,
step 1). For each experiment, 1 mL of PAH solution with
different concentrations was added into 10 mL of the puried
PGMA spheres solution. The mixture was further dialyzed with a
cellulose ester dialysis membrane (MWCO 80 000–100 000) for
24 h to remove the non-adsorbed PAH. The PAH-modied
PGMA cores were separated from the supernatant by centrifugation (30 min at 5000 rpm) followed by cleaning with 11 mL
Nanopure water. The PGMA@PAH dispersion solution was
obtained aer three centrifugation/cleaning cycles and stored
in a refrigerator prior to use. The PAH layer provides a positively
charged surface thereby facilitating subsequent adsorption of
the negatively charged gold nanoparticles (Scheme 1, step 2).

The gold nanoparticles were synthesized with hydrogen tetrachloroaurate trihydrate (HAuCl4$3H2O) as the gold precursor
and trisodium citrate as reductant according to the Turkevich
method.32 In a typical procedure, 250 mL of 1 mM HAuCl4$3H2O
was heated to its boiling point on an IKA RCT DIGITAL Basic
Magnetic Hot Plate Stirrer (Chemglass Life Sciences, Vineland,
NJ, USA), to which 25 mL of 38.3 mM trisodium citrate was
introduced. The heating was continued until the solution
changed from colorless to ruby-red and the solution was
allowed to cool to room temperature. Dialyzed AuNPs were
obtained using a dialysis membrane (MWCO 3500–5000) for
24 h. A certain required amount of PGMA@PAH dispersion
solution was suspended in 3 mL pristine as-synthesized or
dialyzed AuNPs solutions for complete adsorption of AuNPs
through interactions between AuNPs and PGMA@PAH with
opposite charges to form PGMA@PAH@AuNPs in a raspberrylike fashion. To study the pH effect on the gold nanoparticles
surface coverage, the PGMA@PAH solution was prepared by
mixing 10 mL of puried PGMA spheres and 1 mL of 0.5% (w/v)
PAH solution followed by three centrifugation/wash cycles. The centrifuged PGMA@PAH spheres were then dispersed in 11 mL
of Nanopure water with different pH (pH ¼ 3, 5, 7, 9 and 11).
The raspberry-like AuNPs coated PGMA composite spheres were
formed by adding 60 mL of PGMA@PAH dispersion to 3 mL of
the as-synthesized gold nanoparticle solution. All samples were
mixed by gently shaking at around 1000 rpm for 30 min on a SH
2000 Finemixer shaker (Daigger, Vernon Hills, IL, USA). The
PGMA@PAH@AuNPs composites were collected aer centrifugation and re-dispersed in Nanopure water.

Chemicals Highly puried curcumin was gied by Dr P. Srinivas from Central Food Technological Research Institute (CFTRI), Mysore, India. We conrmed the purity using HPLC, NMR and UV-vis spectroscopy (see the ESI†). Ultrapure water (18.2 MU resistance) (Millipore Corp.) was used for all washing procedures as well as for solution preparations. DPPH was obtained from Fluka. All other chemicals and materials were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Chemicals and used as received unless otherwise mentioned.

Synthesis For the synthesis of AuNPs using curcumin, HAuCl4 was added into 500 mL of water to make up a concentration of 1.5  104 M, which was then held at 90 C under constant stirring, followed by the addition of curcumin (4 mg). The high temperature of the reaction enhanced the solubility of curcumin in water and also kinetically controlled the nucleation and growth of the AuNPs so as to achieve a better size distribution of the nanoparticles. Immediately aer mixing the reactants, heating was stopped and the reaction mixture was allowed to cool to room temperature to form a deep red colored AuNP sol with a nal pH of 5.67.

Tetrachloroauric (III) acid (HAuCl4·3H2O (99.9%)), sodium borohydride (NaBH4, 99%), L-ascorbic acid (AA, 99+%), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB, 99%), silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99+%), anhydrous dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), thioglycolic acid (TGA), methyl thioglycolate (MTG), anhydrous hydrazine, 4-dimethylamino pyridine (DMAP), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1,3-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), poly-L-arginine (PLR, Mw: 15–70 kDa), and ethylenediamine-tetraacetic acid (EDTA) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, USA, and used without further purification. Ultrapure deionized water (DI water, Milli-Q Water Systems) was used for all solution preparations and experiments. Glassware used for preparing Au NRs was cleaned by soaking in aqua regia followed by washing with DI water. The heterobifunctional poly (ethylene glycol) (PEG) derivatives, i.e., hydroxyl-PEG-carboxyl (OH-PEG-COOH, Mw: 5000 Da) and methoxyl-PEG-hydroxyl (MPEG-OH, Mw: 5000 Da), were purchased from JenKem Technology, USA. The anticancer drug, doxorubicin·HCl (DOX), was purchased from Tecoland Corporation, USA. Octreotide acetate (Mw: 1019.26 Da) was purchased from Aroz Technologies (USA). All other chemicals and reagents used were of analytical reagent grade. Phosphate and acetate buffered solutions were prepared in our laboratory.

Bare Au NRs were prepared via a well-established two-step seed-mediated growth method with a slight modification.48, 49 Briefly, 600 μl of ice-cold 10 mM NaBH4 solution was quickly added to 250 μl of 0.01 M HAuCl4 dissolved in a 0.1 M CTAB surfactant solution. The solution was stirred for 2 min. Afterwards, it was allowed to react for 2 h to form the CTAB-capped Au nanoparticles to be used as seeds for the synthesis of Au NRs. To synthesize the Au NRs, 95 ml of CTAB solution (0.1 M) was added to a 200 ml flask. Afterwards, 950 μl of AgNO3 solution (0.01 M) mixed in 5 ml of HAuCl4 solution (0.01 M) was added to the CTAB solution. Subsequently, 550 μl of freshly prepared L-ascorbic acid solution (0.1 M) were added to the above mixture as a weak reducing agent. After swirling for 3 min, the gold color disappeared, indicating that the gold (III) was reduced to gold (I). Lastly, 100 μl of CTAB-capped Au nanoparticle seeds were added. The solution was left to stand overnight. The resulting Au NRs were separated from the reaction solution via centrifugation at 14,000 rpm for 12 min, washed with deionized water twice to remove any residual reactants, and separated from water via centrifugation at 14,000 rpm for about 15 min using a Beckman Coulter Avanti J-26-XPI centrifuge (Beckman, Germany).

PLR segments were conjugated onto the Au NRs via an amidation reaction between the amine groups of PLR and the carboxylic acid groups of TGA tethered onto the Au NRs surfaces. Briefly, PLR (0.1 g) was dissolved in 25 ml of 50 mM sodium tetraborate buffer (STBB, pH 8.5). The resulting solution was stirred vigorously for approximately 30 min and subsequently filtered through a 0.22 μm Durapore® membrane (Sterile Millex GV, Sigma–Aldrich, Buchs, Switzerland) into a 45 ml Corning tube. In order to conjugate PLR onto the Au NRs, the carboxyl groups anchored onto the Au NRs were first converted to NHS ester groups via a reaction with NHS. To conduct this reaction, an appropriate stoichiometric amount of functionalized Au NRs (100 mg) and condensing agents EDC (1 mg) and NHS (1 mg) were reacted for 18 h and then slowly added to the above PLR solution while it was continuously stirred. After another 12 h of vigorous stirring at room temperature, the solution was transferred to a dialysis tube (Spectr/Por dialysis tubing, molecular weight cut-off of 8 kDa, Spectrum Laboratories, Inc., Rancho Dominguez, CA). The synthesized product was dialyzed for 24 h in 10 mM phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.0), followed by an additional 24 h of dialysis in deionized water. The product was then freeze dried for 48 h.

DOX was synthesized using a two-step reaction procedure.51 In the first step, the methoxyl groups of the MTG linkers on the surface Au NRs (600 mg) were substituted with hydrazide groups by reacting with 10 mg (0.3 mmol) of anhydrous hydrazine in 50 ml of dry DMF at 40°C for 24 h. Thereafter, the formed intermediate product was dialyzed against a 0.25% ammonia solution for 48 h, followed by freeze drying for 48 h. In the second step, the freeze-dried product was dissolved in 25 ml of DMF, and an excess amount of DOX (60 mg) was added. The mixed solution was stirred in the dark at room temperature for 24 h to obtain DOX conjugated Au NRs. These were then subjected to dialysis against DI water for 72 h to completely remove any un-reacted DOX.

2.1. Synthesis of PEG@AuNPs AuNPs were prepared by the citrate reduction of chloroauric acid according to previously published procedures.14,15 Briefly, 300 ml of 1% chloroauric acid and 30 ml of double-distilled water were mixed and brought to a boil. Then, 600 ml of 1% citric acid was added to the mixture to produce nanoparticles of approximately 14 nm in diameter. The solution was then refluxed for 15 min to obtain the citric acid-stabilized AuNPs. For surface modification, we first synthesized a-lipoyl-umethoxy poly(ethylene glycol) (further denoted as LA-PEGOCH3) according to a method in the literature,16 which is described in detail in the ESI.† Next, LA-PEG-OCH3 was mixed with citric acid-stabilized AuNPs (1 mg) in 10 ml of ultrapurified water. The mixture was stirred for 12 h at room temperature. Thereafter, the nanoparticles were centrifuged at a speed of 14 000 rpm for 10 min to remove unconjugated polymers and washed once with Milli-Q water.

2.1. Synthesis of PEG@AuNPs AuNPs were prepared by the citrate reduction of chloroauric acid according to previously published procedures.14,15 Briefly, 300 ml of 1% chloroauric acid and 30 ml of double-distilled water were mixed and brought to a boil. Then, 600 ml of 1% citric acid was added to the mixture to produce nanoparticles of approximately 14 nm in diameter. The solution was then refluxed for 15 min to obtain the citric acid-stabilized AuNPs. For surface modification, we first synthesized a-lipoyl-umethoxy poly(ethylene glycol) (further denoted as LA-PEGOCH3) according to a method in the literature,16 which is described in detail in the ESI.† Next, LA-PEG-OCH3 was mixed with citric acid-stabilized AuNPs (1 mg) in 10 ml of ultrapurified water. The mixture was stirred for 12 h at room temperature. Thereafter, the nanoparticles were centrifuged at a speed of 14 000 rpm for 10 min to remove unconjugated polymers and washed once with Milli-Q water.

Synthesis of Au@SiO2 nanoparticle solution The Au@SiO2 nanoparticle solution was synthesized according to previously published methods29–31 with slight modifications. Bare Au nanoparticles are synthesized by the reduction of hydrogen tetrachloroaurate(III) (HAuCl4$3H2O). Then, 0.01%w/w tetrachloroaurate(III) aqueous solution (100 mL) was heated and citric acid solution (1% w/w, 4 mL) was quickly injected into the boiling solution. The solution quickly turned colorless then blue and lastly rose-colored. The solution was left boiling for 6 min and was then removed from the heat source. Subsequently, citrate-stabilized Au nanoparticle solution was successfully prepared. The formation of silica-coated Au nanoparticles started with replacing citrate on the surface of the nanoparticles with 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES): APTES aqueous solution (2 mL, 1.71 mM) was added to the prepared Au nanoparticle solution under vigorous stirring. To obtain silica-coated Au nanoparticles, 0.54% w/w sodium silicate solution (4 mL) was added 40 min after adding APTES. The final solution was vigorously stirred for about 12 min and then allowed to sit for 3 d. The pH of the sodium silicate solution was adjusted to 10–11 before addition to the Au solution.

Chemicals and instrumentation Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), thiourea, NaBH4, trisodium citrate, tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (TRIS), polyvinylalcohol (PVA), dithiozone, octadecylamine, o-aminophenol and potassium thiocyanate were obtained from SRL Chemicals, India and used as received. All the apparatus were rinsed with aqua regia (3 : 1 solution of HCl : HNO3) and then washed with double distilled water before use. All reagents and solvents used in this study were of guaranteed reagent grade

Synthesis of citrate stabilized AuNPs AuNPs were freshly prepared by the standard citrate reduction method.51 In a typical experiment, 2.5 ml of trisodium citrate (38.8 mM) was rapidly injected into a boiling solution of HAuCl4 (25 ml, 1 mM), and the mixed solution was further refluxed for another 25 min into a wine-red suspension. The suspension was gradually cooled to room temperature under stirring for another 30 min, and stored at 4 C for further use.

Synthesis of TRIS stabilized AuNPs TRIS stabilized AuNPs were prepared by the reduction of HAuCl4 with NaBH4. 47 In a typical synthesis, 0.1 ml of an aqueous solution of 1% HAuCl4 was added to 10 ml of water containing 0.5 mM TRIS and was stirred for 2 min. Then, 0.25 ml of an aqueous solution of NaBH4 (0.08%) was added dropwise under stirring. The stirring was continued for another 10 min, and the resulting nanocolloid was stored at 4 C

Synthesis of PVA stabilized AuNPs PVA-stabilized gold sol was prepared according to a reported method.52 To a 2 wt% PVA solution in 130 ml of water, 5.6 ml of 1 mM HAuCl4 was added and stirred for 5 min. A quantity of 1.3 ml of 0.1 M aqueous NaBH4 solution was added to the above solution under vigorous stirring to give rise to colloidal AuNPs.

Synthesis of amine stabilized AuNPs A stock solution (1 mM) of octadecylamine (ODA) was made in DMSO by dissolving in hot conditions. A quantity of 15 ml of 1 mM HAuCl4 was reduced by 1 ml of 1 mM ODA under stirring and heating at 80 C to yield ruby red AuNPs.

Synthesis of biogenic AuNPs Plant-based AuNPs were prepared according to a recently reported method.2 To a solution containing 6 ml of 1 mM HAuCl4 and 3.5 ml of water, 0.5 ml of the stem extract of Breynia rhamnoides was added to yield stable ruby red AuNPs stabilized by plant metabolites.

Synthesis of AuNPs by laser ablation Pure gold metal foil of dimension (2 cm  1 cm) was ultrasonically cleaned and placed in a 25 ml glass beaker containing 3 ml of millipore water. The gold strip was irradiated with the fundamental harmonic (1064 nm) of a high power nanosecond Nd:YAG (Surelite III) laser. The intense laser pulse was optically steered and focused onto the gold foil using a convex lens (focal length ¼ 10 cm). The laser pulse energy (50 mJ) and the repetition rate of 10 Hz were kept constant throughout the ablation period. The surface chemistry of laser ablated AuNPs is reported to be dominated by negatively charged functionalities like Au, AuO, AuOH, and CO3 Au, thus accounting for electrostatic stabilization.53

Synthesis of TU derivatives All the derivatives of TU are prepared according to a reported method.54 Briefly, o-hydroxyphenylthiourea (720 mg, 47%, white solid) (OHPTU) was prepared by taking 0.017 moles of o-aminophenol in 15 ml of ethanol, stirred at room temperature while 2.14 ml of 37.4% concentrated hydrochloric acid was added dropwise. The suspension formed was heated to reflux. After all the suspension had dissolved, 0.0255 moles of potassium thiocyanate in 5 ml of ethanol was added to the reaction mixture and was stirred at reflux for 18 hours. The precipitate formed upon cooling was dried under vacuum and recrystallized from ethanol. The other TU derivatives like phenylthiourea (PTU), p-nitrophenylthiourea (PNPTU), and benzoylthiourea (BTU) were similarly prepared from their respective starting materials.

Chloroauric acid tetrahydrate (HAuCl4$4H2O, 99.95%), oleylamine (OAm, 80%), elemental sulfur, 1-dodecanethiol (DDT,
98%) and tert-dodecanethiol (t-DDT, 98%) were purchased from
Aladdin reagent company. Octadecene (ODE, 90%), copper(II)
acetylacetonate (Cu(acac)2, 99.99%) and copper(II) chloride
anhydrous (CuCl2, 99.99%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich.
All chemicals are analytical grade and were used as received.
Deionized water was used in all cases.

Synthesis of alloy AuCu nanocrystals AuCu alloy nanocrystals were synthesized by seed based diffusion method similar to the report by Li et al.45 Briefly, 8 nm Au seed nanoparticles were first prepared by reducing HAuCl4 with OAm. An aqueous solution of HAuCl4 (1 M, 70 mL) was dissolved in 5 mL OAm at room temperature, and the solution was then quickly heated to 150 C under nitrogen with vigorous stirring. After two hours, the reaction was stopped and the solution was cooled down to room temperature naturally. Au nanoparticles were precipitated by adding ethanol to the reaction mixture and then separated by centrifugation. The obtained nanoparticles were washed by hexane and ethanol twice, and were then redispersed in 1 mL hexane for subsequent steps. For the synthesis of alloy AuCu NPs, Cu(acac)2 (20 mg, 0.07 mmol) and pre-made 8 nm Au (0.07 mmol) NPs were added in 5 mL OAm. The reaction mixture was thoroughly mixed and fluxed under nitrogen for 30 min at room temperature, which was then heated to 120 C at a ramp rate of 6 C min1 . After holding the reactants at 120 C for 30 min to remove water, the temperature was further raised to 250 C at a rate of 2 C min1 . Reaction was allowed to continue at 250 C for 70 min, after which the solution was cooled down to room temperature. AuCu nanoparticles were precipitated by adding ethanol and centrifuged at 8500 rpm for 8 min. After washing with hexane and ethanol, the nanoparticles were redispersed in hexane for further use

Synthesis of Au–Cu2S heterodimer nanostructures using DDT Pre-made AuCu (0.07 mmol) nanoparticles dispersed in 1 mL hexane were mixed with 3 mL ODE and the solution was heated to 85 C to remove hexane under air. 1 hour later, the temperature was raised to 180 C at a rate of 4 C min1 . After reaching this temperature, 2 mL DDT was added dropwise into the solution, and the solution color quickly changed to light green. 5 min later, the solution was cooled to room temperature. For X-ray diffraction analysis, the obtained products were washed with hexane and ethanol once. For nanoparticle morphology characterizations, the obtained products were washed three times with chloroform and ethanol.

Synthesis of Au–Cu2S heterodimer nanostructures using elemental sulfur 0.07 mmol AuCu nanoparticles dispersed in 1 mL hexane solution were mixed with 3 mL ODE and heated to 80 C to remove hexane under nitrogen. After 30 min, the solution was further heated to 100 C. 10 mg sulfur dissolved in toluene was dropwise added into the solution. The solution was left to react for 10 min and then cooled to room temperature. The obtained NPs went through the same washing steps as above for further use and study.

Synthesis of Cu(I)–DDT complex Cu(I)–DDT complex was prepared by reacting anhydrous copper(II) chloride with DDT. In a typical synthesis, 200 mL DDT was added dropwise into 10 mg CuCl2, and the initial brown powder immediately changed to grey and finally formed a gellike complex. For further use, an extra 1.8 mL DDT was added to disperse the complex.

Gold nanorods were prepared using the improved seed-mediated growth method.15 The synthesis steps are as follows: firstly, the seed solutions were prepared by the reduction of auric acid in the presence of (1-hexadecyl)trimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) by mixing 5 mL of 0.20 M CTAB with 5.0 mL of 5  104 M HAuCl4, followed by stirring. Then 0.6 mL ice-cold sodium borohydride (0.01 M) was added quickly with vigorous stirring of the seed solution for another 2 min, which resulted in the formation of a brownish yellow solution. The growth solution was prepared by adding 0.22 mL of 0.004 M AgNO3 solution (the aspect ratio of gold nanorods can be controlled by adding AgNO3 in the reaction37) to 5 mL of a mixed solution of 0.2 M CTAB and 0.15 M benzylcetyldimethylammonium chloride hydrate (BDAC). To this solution, 5.0 mL of 0.001 M HAuCl4 was added and the solution was stirred. This was followed by the addition of 60 mL of 0.0788 M ascorbic acid, which changed the color of the growth solution from dark yellow to colorless. Finally, 12 mL of the seed solution was added to the growth solution, and over a period of 3–4 hours, the seeds grew into nanorods.

Then 0.6 mL ice-cold sodium borohydride (0.01 M) was added quickly with vigorous stirring of the seed solution for another 2 min, which resulted in the formation of a brownish yellow solution. The synthesis of spherical ended gold nanodumbbells was performed by the addition of 1.5 mL water to 0.5 mL of the Au nanorods seed solution, followed by the addition of different amounts of 0.1 M NaOH solution sequentially. The absorption spectra of the samples were analyzed immediately. The longitudinal surface plasmon resonance peak of the gold nanorods was blue shifted on addition of NaOH solution. NaOH solution was gradually added until the position of the longitudinal surface plasmon resonance peak did not change

Arrow ended Au nanodumbbells A similar experimental procedure was carried out for the synthesis of the arrow ended Au nanodumbbells. The synthesis process involved addition of 1.5 mL water to 0.5 mL of the Au nanorods solution, and then different amounts of ascorbic (0.0788 M) acid solution were mixed into the solution sequentially, after which the absorption spectrum of the sample was taken to measure the shift in the longitudinal surface plasmon resonance peak. Ascorbic acid was added until the position of the longitudinal surface plasmon resonance peak did not shift

1. Materials Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4$3H2O), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), silver nitrate (AgNO3), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), L-ascorbic acid (AA), N,N0 -methylenebisacrylamide (MBA), 3-(trimethoxylsilyl) propyl methacrylate (MPS) and Nhydroxymethyl acrylamide (NHMA) were purchased from Fluka and used as received, and N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAM) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich and recrystallized from a toluene–hexane solvent. Tetraethyoxysilane (TEOS), ammonium nitrate (NH4NO3) and potassium persulfate (KPS) were obtained from Shanghai Chemical Reagents Company. Other chemicals were of reagent grade and were used without further purification. DOX in the form of the hydrochloride salt was obtained from Bejing Huafeng United Technology Company. MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay and other biological reagents were purchased from Invitrogen Corp. Milli-Q grade water was used in all preparations

2. Gold nanorod synthesis Gold nanorods (GNRs) were prepared by a seed-mediated growth method.20 Briefly, 600 mL ice-cold 0.01 M NaBH4 was mixed with 10 mL of an aqueous solution containing 0.1 M CTAB and 100 mL 1% HAuCl4 under vigorous stirring. After the solution was stirred, it was kept at 25 C for at least 2 h before further use as the seed solution. The growth solution contained 40 mL 0.2 M CTAB, 2 mL 0.01 M HAuCl4, 750 mL 0.01 M AgNO3 and 320 mL 0.01 M ascorbic acid; after gently mixing the growth solution, 96 mL seed solution was added to it and the mixture was held at 28 C for 6 h to obtain gold nanorods.

The mesoporous silica coating was achieved by a modified Stober € method.17 First, the obtained gold nanorods were washed with Milli-Q water twice to remove excess CTAB and redispersed in 40 mL Milli-Q water. Subsequently, 50 mL aqueous ammonia solution (25 wt%) was added to adjust the pH value to about 10, then 10.5 mL 10 mM TEOS–ethanol solution was added to the system at the rate of 3.5 mL h1 . The mixture was allowed to react at 40 C for 24 h under gentle agitation. The synthesized product was centrifuged, washed with Milli-Q water and ethanol several times. Then, the above CTAB-containing product was added directly into 40 mL of an ethanol solution containing 100 mL of MPS. After being stirred at 25 C for 12 h, the template CTAB was removed in NH4NO3–ethanol (60 mL, 10 mg mL1 ) solution through ion exchange. The mixture was refluxed at 80 C for 6 h, and then collected by centrifugation and repeatedly washed with ethanol. The obtained MPS-modified mesoporous silica-coated GNRs (GNR@mSiO2) were redispersed in Milli-Q water and diluted to 1 wt% for further use.

The smart polymer shell was synthesized by precipitation polymerization in the presence of MPS-modified mesoporous silicacoated GNRs.21,22 In a typical procedure, an aqueous solution of 3.6 mL 300 mM NIPAM, 3 mL 100 mM NHMA (the content of NHMA was 15 wt%, which was used in all measurements except when otherwise stated), 835 mL 50 mM MBA (5% crosslinking density), 2 mL Milli-Q water and 2 mL 1 wt% MPS-modified mesoporous silica-coated GNRs was added to a three-necked flask equipped with a reflux condenser. The mixture was heated to 75 C and kept under a nitrogen atmosphere. After mechanical stirring at 300 rpm for 0.5 h, 800 mL 10 mM KPS solution was rapidly added and the polymerization was allowed to proceed for another 4 h. Finally, the obtained product (GNR@mSiO2/ P(NIPAM-co-NHMA)) composite nanoparticles were collected by centrifugation and washed with Milli-Q water to remove the unreacted monomers.

2.2. Synthesis of pyrenyl dextran As described previously, pyrenyl dextran was formed via conjugation of the hydroxyl group of dextran (Mw: 10 000 Da) with the carboxyl group of 1-pyrenebutyric acid (Mw: 288.34 Da) using DCC and DMAP (Fig. 2 and 3).33–35 Specifically, dextran (0.1 mmol), 1-pyrenebutyric acid (4.2 mmol), DCC (0.6 mmol), DMAP (0.6 mmol), and TEA (0.2 mmol) were dissolved in a vial containing 60 mL of DMSO. The solution was reacted for 48 h at room temperature under a nitrogen atmosphere and then lyophilized. Afterwards, the mixture of reactants was dissolved in excess ultrapure deionized water, filtered through a 200 nm pore
size cellulose acetate filter (Advantec) to remove the byproduct,
and then dialyzed using a dialysis membrane (MWCO: 3500 Da)
against an excess of an aqueous phase over seven days. The
purified product was freeze-dried and stored under vacuum for
later use. The characteristic bands and chemical structure of the
synthesized pyrenyl dextran were confirmed utilizing Fouriertransform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Varian, Excalibur
series) and 1H-NMR (400 MHz, Varian INOVA400 NMR
spectrometer) using DMSO-d6 as a solvent, respectively. The
absorbance and fluorescence intensity of the pyrenyl groups in
pyrenyl dextran were determined by using a UV-vis spectrophotometer (Optizen 2120UV, MECASYS Co) and a fluorescence spectrometer (FP-6500, JASCO), respectively. In addition,
the electrochemical potential of pyrenyl dextran as a reducing
agent was determined by using an electrochemical analyzer (CH
Instruments Inc, CHI624B). Electrochemical studies were conducted using a three-electrode system with a 0.1 M solution of
TBAP (tetrabutylammonium perchlorate) in anhydrous dimethylacetamide (DMAc) as the supporting electrolyte. The
working electrode was a platinum electrode, the counter electrode was a platinum wire, and the reference electrode was
Ag/AgCl. Deoxygenation of the solutions was achieved by
bubbling with argon for approximately 20 min.38

For the synthesis of gold nanoparticles
using pyrenyl dextran as a reducible stabilizer, which could
simultaneously act as a stabilizer and reducing agent, 50 mg of
pyrenyl dextran was dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water and
boiled at 95 C under vigorous stirring. Then, 1 mL of a 1 wt%
AuCl4 solution was quickly injected and the solution was kept
under stirring at 95 C until the color of the solution changed
from light yellow to deep violet. The heat source was removed
after 24 h and the solution was cooled naturally to room temperature. The excess pyrenyl dextran was removed by
repetitive centrifugation and washing with deionized water while
the precipitated DGNPs were re-dispersed in 10 mL of deionized

2.1. Chemicals and reagents Aniline, hydrogen tetrachloroaurate(III) trihydrate, ammonium persulfate, trisodium citrate and camphorosulfonic acid were used as received from Sigma-Aldrich. PSA antibody (anti-PSA) was procured from AbD Serotec. All other chemicals were of analytical grade and were used without further purification.

2.2. Synthesis of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) were prepared using the citrate reduction method, wherein 20 mL of 1 mM HAuCl4 solution were refluxed with continuous stirring. When the solution begins to boil 2 mL of 25 mM trisodium citrate is added rapidly to the solution. The solution is allowed to boil for another 15 minutes during which the color of the solution changes from pale yellow to deep red. The change of color to deep red signifies formation of the AuNPs after which the solution is cooled to room temperature with stirring. The size of the AuNPs is characterized using UV-visible and TEM studies (Fig. 1).

2.3. Synthesis and electrophoretic deposition of polyaniline
(PANI) nanowires
Nanostructured polyaniline (PANI) was synthesized chemically
in the emeraldine salt form by oxidative polymerization of
aniline in acidic media using ammonium persulphate (APS) as
the oxidant and camphorosulfonic acid (CSA) as the dopant.
Aniline monomer (0.05 M) dissolved in the acidic media (1 M
HCl and CSA 0.75 M) and APS (0.05 mol) were added drop wise
to the above solution for 5 h at 0 C and left over night at room
temperature for complete polymerization. A green precipitate
obtained after the filtration was dispersed in 1 M NH4OH to
convert it to emeraldine base (EB) form followed by CH3OH
washing to remove oligomers. Further washing was done with
deionized water and kept at 60 C for 24 h for drying.31
Thin films of nanostructured PANI were electrophoretically
deposited onto a Au/Cr/SiO2/Si substrate employing a colloidal
suspension of PANI nanowires in acetonitrile. For preparation
of the nanostructured PANI colloidal suspension (2 mL) in
acetonitrile, 100 mL of stock solution (1 mg mL1
) of emeraldine
base in formic acid was dispersed in 1.9 mL of acetonitrile followed by ultrasonification for 1 min.18 The Au/Cr/SiO2/Si electrodes (25 mm  5 mm) are prepared by Au/Cr (100 nm/10 nm)
deposition on thermally oxidized p-type h100i Si wafers using
E-beam evaporation followed by dicing. Before deposition of
Au/Cr, wafers are cleaned using the standard Radio Corporation
of America (RCA) cleaning protocol. Briefly, RCA involves two
cleaning solutions namely SC-1 and SC-2 (SC stands for Standard Clean). SC-1 is composed of NH4OH + H2O2 + H2O
(1 : 1 : 6) and SC-2 is made up of HCl + H2O2 + H2O (1 : 1 : 6).
Both the solutions are heated to a temperature of 60 C. In the
cleaning procedure wafers are first immersed in the SC-1 solution
for 10 min. This removes trace organic impurities present on the
wafer surface. The wafers are then rinsed with DI water. This
procedure also forms a thin layer of oxide on the wafer surface,
which is removed by immersing the wafers in HF + DI water
(1 : 50) for around 20 s. The wafers are rinsed again in DI water and then immersed in the SC-2 solution for 10 min. This solution
helps in removing trace metal impurities. The wafers are then
rinsed again with DI water and dried using a N2 coupled spin
dryer.
The Au/Cr/SiO2/Si electrode is then used as the anode in a twoelectrode cell assembly while gold foil (30 mm  10 mm) is used
as the cathode. The two electrodes, placed parallel to each other
with a separation of 5 mm between them, were dipped in the
nano-structured PANI suspension. Film deposition time and
voltage were optimized and a stable response was observed for
the films (25 mm2
) deposited at 100 V for 60 s. The films were
then removed from the suspension, washed with deionized water
and dried with nitrogen.

For the preparation of hollow microfibers, the mixture was kept in the dark for about two weeks to avoid photo-oxidation of Den 40 until large aggregates were observed. 12  2 nm gold nanoparticles were synthesized through the reduction of hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (III) (HAuCl4) in the presence of sodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7$2H2O) according to the procedure published by Grabar et al.29 The gold nanoparticles were coated with a 12  2 nm fluorescent silica shell according to the procedure published by Graf et al.30 Briefly, 0.15 g of polyvinylpolypyrrolidone (PVP K15, Mw 10000) in 5 ml of water was added to 100 ml of the aqueous solution of gold nanoparticles (2.71  1015 particles l1 ). The reaction occurred at room temperature in the dark under continuous stirring for 12 h. PVP-capped gold nanoparticles were collected by centrifugation (16000 rpm for 1 h) and redispersed in 10 mL of ethanol. 100 mL of an ethanolic solution of tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS, 0.2 mM)) and C500 (7.7  103 mM) or DCM (103 mM) were then added to this solution. The reactive medium was stirred in the dark for two hours before the addition of 10 ml of ammonia (25% in water). The reaction occurred at room temperature in the dark under continuous stirring for over 24 h. The core–shell particles were collected and washed through three cycles of centrifugation (16000 rpm for 1 h at room temperature) and redispersed in absolute ethanol. At the end of the washing cycles, the fluorescence of the supernatant was measured. No significant signal was detected, indicating that dye leaching to solution did not occur.

Gold and silica nanoparticle synthesis Gold nanorods were prepared using the standard seed-mediated method in the presence of the cationic surfactant cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB). Gold seeds were prepared by mixing an aqueous CTAB solution (0.2 M, 5.0 mL) with 0.5 mM NaAuCl4 (5.0 mL) prior to the addition of ice-cold 0.01 M NaBH4 (0.6 mL). A growth solution was prepared by mixing CTAB (0.2 M, 10.0 mL) with 1.0 mM NaAuCl4 (10.0 mL) and various amounts of AgNO3 (0.1 M). Ascorbic acid (78.8 mM, 1.4 mL) was then added as a mild reducing agent, which resulted in the disappearance of the colour of the growth solution. The brownish-yellow seed solution was added under strong agitation to the growth solution. The reaction was left to proceed for 24 h at 28 C and the rod solutions were purified by centrifugation at 7000 rpm for 10 min and resuspended in Milli-Q water. Extinction coefficients determined experimentally by Orendorff and Murphy12 were used to calculate the concentration of gold nanorods. Gold nanoparticles were synthesized using a standard method based on the citrate reduction of gold chloride. A 1% trisodium citrate solution in water was added to a 0.01% solution of NaAuCl4 under reflux and kept under strong agitation. The gold nanoparticles were aminated by coating a poly(allylamine hydrochloride) layer onto the citrate–gold nanoparticles as previously described.13

Monodisperse silica nanoparticles were synthesized by combining sol–gel chemistry and emulsion polymerization as described previously.14,15 Poly(allylamine hydrochloride) was used to functionalize the silica nanoparticles with reactive amino groups as follows. The polymer was dissolved in Milli-Q water at 2 mg mL1 and the pH adjusted to 8.5 with NaOH. The nanoparticle suspension was slowly added to the PAH solution under strong agitation. The mixture was kept under sonication for 5 min and then left to stand for 55 min. The mixture was then distributed in 1.5 mL Eppendorf tubes and centrifuged for 30 min at 13 400 rpm. The supernatants were removed and the nanoparticles resuspended in H2O. The washing procedure was repeated 3 times to remove unbound PAH.

Gold nanorods were PEGylated using PEG–SH. Equi-volume solutions of purified gold nanorods in H2O (0.25 nM) and PEG– SH (1 mg mL1 in H2O) were mixed under vigorous stirring, sonicated for 30 s and left to react for 2 h. In the standard protocol, excess PEG molecules were then removed by centrifugation at 7000 rpm for 10 min, the supernatant discarded and the PEGylated rods either resuspended in water or 1 PBS. In the two-step process designed here to yield high density PEG layers, the PEGylated nanorods resuspended in 1 PBS were mixed with a fresh PEG–SH solution (1.5 mg mL1 ) in 1 PBS supplemented with K2SO4 (0.6 M) in a 1 : 2 ratio to yield a 0.4 M K2SO4 solution. The samples were placed in a heating bath and the temperature gradually increased by 5 C every 5 min until it reached 50 C. The samples were left to react for an additional 30 min at 50 C, after which the temperature was lowered back to room temperature. The PEGylated nanorod solution was sonicated for 30 s and excess PEG–SH was removed by centrifugation as above. To investigate the efficacy of the 2-step method for the covalent grafting of PEG, PAH-coated gold and silica nanoparticles were reacted with PEG–NHS ester using a similar protocol as for PEG–SH on gold.

Gold nanorods (GNRs) were prepared according to the seedmediated growth method by EI-Sayed and co-workers.1 Briefly, a seed solution was prepared by mixing 7.5 mL of CTAB (0.1 M) and 2.5 mL of HAuCl4 (1 mM) with 0.6 mL freshly prepared 10 mM NaHB4 solution under vigorous stirring at 28 °C. After 2 h, this seed solution was used for the synthesis of the GNRs. In a flask, 13.2 mL of 0.1 M CTAB was mixed with 12 mL of 1 mM HAuCl4, then 240 μL of 10 mM silver nitrate aqueous solution and 220 μL of 2 M hydrochloric acid were added to the flask. After gently mixing the solution, 192 μL of 0.1 M ascorbic acid was added. On continuously stirring this mixture, various concentrations of seed solution were added finally to initiate the growth of the GNRs. These nanorods were aged for 5 h to ensure full growth at 28 °C. After preparation, excess CTAB was removed by centrifuging twice at 12 000 rpm for 10 min, and then redispersed in DI water. PAA coated GNRs were prepared by the versatile layer-bylayer (LBL) approach. In brief, 2 mg mL−1 PAA-450 000 g mol−1 was prepared in a 10 mM NaCl solution (which was sonicated previously for 30 min). Then 2 mL GNRs (2×) were added dropwise to the PAA solution (v/v = 1 : 1), and the mixture was
stirred vigorously for 6 h. The PAA coated GNRs were separated
by centrifugation three times to remove excess polyelectrolyte and
then redispersed in 2 mL of PBS (pH 7.4). PAA-15 000 g mol−1
coated gold nanorods were prepared according to the method by
Michael D. Wyatt.37

We employed 18 and 40 nm spherical, citrate-capped gold nanoparticles, as seeds for the growing of bigger MBNPs. Gold seeds of 18 nm were prepared by the classical Turkevich–Frens method,41,42 using trisodium citrate as a reducing agent in aqueous solution. Briefly, 150 mL of 0.25 mM aqueous solution of HAuCl4 (Sigma-Aldrich) was heated to boiling while stirring, then 1.15 mL of 0.1 M aqueous solution of sodium citrate (Sigma-Aldrich) was added. The solution was kept gently boiling until a red wine color appeared and then slowly cooled down. Gold seeds of ca. 40 nm were prepared according to a two-step seed-mediated method proposed by Brown and Natan26 which allows the enlargement of 18 nm AuNPs (seeds) for the property of NH2OH to efficiently reduce Au3+ to bulk metal in the presence of Au surface.25 The synthesis was performed by adding 1.2 mL of aqueous 0.1 M hydroxylamine sulfate (Sigma-Aldrich) and an appropriate amount of 18 nm gold seeds into 250 mL of water. The solution was kept under vigorous stirring and then 20 mL of 2 mM aqueous solution of HAuCl4 was dropwise added to the seeds solution (1 mL min1 ). After the addition of HAuCl4 solution was over, stirring was continued for 30 min and then sodium citrate was injected to a final concentration of 1 mM to stabilize the colloidal solution.

To prepare thorny star-shaped gold nanoparticles, a gold/silver solution was obtained by mixing 20 mL 10 mM aqueous HAuCl4 and 2 mL 10 mM aqueous AgNO3 in 1 mL Milli-Q water. After the solution had been thoroughly mixed, 4 mL of aqueous 100 mM ascorbic acid was quickly added, and the solution was pipetted and shaken for 20 s. It changed from colorless to light blue as the reducing agent was added, indicating the formation of thorny gold star nanomaterials. The final pH was 3.0. Changing the molar ratio of HAuCl4 : AgNO3 led to the star-shaped nanoparticles with reduced thorns and irregularly quasi-spherical gold nanoparticles. The synthetic specification of various molar ratios of HAuCl4 to AgNO3 is provided in Fig. S1.†

Modified chitosan (O-carboxymethylchitosan) was modified as described elsewhere.24 5 g of chitosan with low molecular weight was stirred vigorously in 50 mL 50 wt% NaOH solution for 48 h. After yellowish white powders were obtained by passing the solution through a filter (internal diameter: 95 mm; external diameter: 105 mm), the supernatant was dried at 60 C for 3 h. The alkalized chitosan was transferred to a three-necked roundbottomed flask with a volume of 100 mL; it was then mixed with 10 g monochloroacetic acid/25 mL isopropanol, which were added dropwise over 15 min and left to react for 24 h. The modified chitosan was collected by filtration. Aqueous 1 wt% modified chitosan solution was prepared as a stabilizer to stabilize the shape of the star-shaped NPs and maintain effective dispersion in the aqueous solution. The aqueous 1 wt% modified chitosan was added to a solution of as-prepared gold nanoparticles, yielding a total solution of 2 mL. This solution was centrifuged (9279 g) three times for 10 min each time, and redispersed into 2 mL D.I. water for further usage.

FITC-labeled chitosan was prepared as described elsewhere.25 In short, 1 mg of FITC/1 mL dehydrated methanol was added to 1 mL 1 wt% modified chitosan solution with which it reacted in the dark for three hours. The FITC-labeled chitosan was precipitated using 0.1 M NaOH, increasing the solution pH to 10. Unreacted FITC was washed by centrifugation with continued redispersal in dehydrated methanol until the supernatant exhibited no fluorescence by UV irradiation detection.
The surfaces of the FITC-labeled chitosan gold nanoparticles
were modified using the procedure described above

The sulfonated graphene precursor was prepared by directly anchoring sulfonic acid-containing aryl radicals to the surface of reduced graphene.19 Then, the –SO3H groups were reduced by lithium aluminum hydride (AlLiH4) after chlorosulfonylation.20 In brief, the prepared sulfonated graphene (60 mg) was added to a mixture of toluene (15 mL) and thionyl chloride (4 mL). Several drops of dimethylformamide (DMF) were then added as a catalyst and the mixture refluxed for 10 h to prepare the chlorosulfonylated graphene. The resulting mixture was washed with tetrahydrofuran (THF) several times and the chlorosulfonylated graphene finally dispersed in THF (15 mL). Lithium aluminum hydride (0.5 g) was then added and refluxed for 8 h. After the reaction was complete, the reaction was quenched with 1 M HCl, and the resulting suspension was centrifuged and washed several times with 1 M HCl to remove the Al and Li ions. The yielded suspension was dialyzed for 48 h to obtain a purified sulfhydrylated graphene suspension. The sulfhydrylated graphene was placed in an oxygen free environment to avoid oxidization.

The cubic and rod structured gold particles were grown from spherical Au nanoparticles, which acted as seeds, according to research described by Sau et al. and Nikoobakht et al., respectively.21,22 In summary, Au seeds were prepared by the reduction of HAuCl4 (0.25 mL, 10 mM) by ice-cooled NaBH4 (0.6 mL, 10 mM) in the presence of CTAB (7.5 mL, 0.1 M). Au nanocubes were formed in a growth solution (10 mL) containing CTAB (1.6 mL, 0.1 M), HAuCl4 (0.4 mL, 10 mM), AgNO3 (60 mL, 10 mM), ascorbic acid (64 mL, 0.1 M) and Au seeds (5 mL, 2.5  105 M). Au nanorods were formed in a growth solution containing CTAB (4.75 mL, 0.1 M), HAuCl4 (0.2 mL, 10 mM), AgNO3 (60 mL, 5 mM), HCl (0.5 mL, 0.1 M), ascorbic acid (32 mL, 0.1 M) and pre-prepared Au seeds (10 mL). Spherical gold nanoparticles were prepared in a microwave reactor. Briefly, a trisodium citrate solution (2.5 mL, 38.8 mM) was added to de-ionized water (20 mL) with vigorous stirring; then, a HAuCl4 solution (0.25 mL, 10 mM) was added and reacted in the microwave reactor (373 K, 300 W) for 10 min.

Ag particles were prepared by the reduction of AgNO3. In brief, AgNO3 (0.25 mL, 10 mM) was added to de-ionized water (20 mL) containing a trisodium citrate solution (2 mL, 38.8 mM). After stirring for 30 min, ice-cooled NaBH4 (1.0 mL, 10 mM) was added dropwise to the solution. Fe3O4 particles were prepared by a modified co-precipitation method.23 FeCl3 (0.2 mL, 10 mM) and FeCl2 (0.1 mL, 10 mM) were added to de-ionized water (20 mL). Under ultrasonic and gas protective conditions, an ammonia solution (1 mL, 25%) was added dropwise. All of the prepared nanoparticles were centrifuged and washed with de-ionized water several times to remove stabilizer and unreacted metal ions.
